name: Lua
summary: Embeddable scripting language whose modules are simply tables returned by executed files; the language imposes minimal structure and leaves loading policy to the host or application — a stripped-down version of the runtime-import model focused on embeddability.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Lua (Roberto Ierusalimschy et al., 1993+) is a small embeddable scripting language; its module system is one of the lightest in mainstream production. Bytecode and JIT concerns live in `data/luajit-bytecode`, `data/luajit-ir`, `data/lua-generational-gc`, `data/luau-native`. This record covers the language-level module-system surface.

sections.title: Modules as tables returned by files
sections.content:
Lua traditionally treats modules as **tables returned by executed files**. `require 'foo'` searches for `foo.lua` (or a compiled binary module) along the configured paths, executes it, captures the returned value (typically a table), caches it in `package.loaded`, and returns it.

This is in some ways an even more stripped-down version of Python's loader idea: a module is whatever namespace-like object the file computes and returns. There is no language-level `module` keyword, no enforced encapsulation, no built-in distinction between public and private members of the module table.

sections.title: Embeddability as a design priority
sections.content:
For embeddable systems, Lua's minimal module discipline is excellent — the language imposes little structure and leaves policy to the host or the application. Game engines, configuration scripting, plugin systems, and embedded-device runtimes can all impose their own loading and visibility conventions on top of the bare `require`/`package.loaded` mechanism.

The cost is that modularity becomes a **convention, not a strong semantic substrate**. For a language whose primary use case is embedding, this is the right trade-off: a language designed to live inside other systems should not impose strong opinions about what counts as a module. For a stand-alone application language, the lack of structure is more often a liability than an asset.
